policy_statement: Apply sustainable yield parameters so extractions do not exceed forest growth under I4 (I4b).
mechanism_description: Applying sustainable yield parameters keeps forest extractions from exceeding forest growth, helping preserve forest carbon stocks by avoiding overharvesting.
primary_causal_pathway: Forest management limits → ↓ biomass removal/forest degradation → ↑/preserved ecosystem carbon stocks → ↓ net CO₂ in atmosphere
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Forest harvesting activity is constrained via sustainable yield rules so removals do not exceed regrowth. (2) Mediating process: Reduced extraction lowers degradation and maintains standing biomass and associated soil/woody carbon pools, sustaining ongoing sequestration capacity. (3) Outcome: Avoided CO₂ emissions (from lost biomass/soil carbon) and preserved carbon sink function, yielding lower net atmospheric CO₂.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is preserving forest carbon stocks (avoiding CO₂ emissions and foregone sequestration) by preventing overharvesting.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing forests to keep harvest below growth primarily affects climate via ecosystem carbon storage (maintained biomass/soil carbon and continued sequestration).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While sustainable harvest can also support resource efficiency (steady timber supply) or reduce risk (e.g., erosion), the largest climate-relevant effect is the avoided emissions/maintained sink from preserving forest carbon stocks—an NBS pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If harvest limits are justified mainly for biodiversity/watershed reasons, the climate effect still operates through carbon stock preservation; if paired with substitution claims (wood products displacing steel/concrete), that would be an additional mitigation pathway but not the dominant mechanism stated here.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that caps harvest at sustainable yield shares the same core carbon-stock-preservation mechanism regardless of jurisdiction.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., avoided deforestation vs. improved forest management) is deferred to policy-level evidence and accounting specifics.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implementation increases harvest relative to a lower baseline (e.g., formalizing higher allowable cuts) or shifts logging leakage elsewhere, net carbon benefits could be reduced; classification remains NBS because the intended/structural mechanism is carbon stock preservation via improved forest management.
key_indicators: “Sustainable yield parameters”; “guaranteeing that extractions do not exceed forest growth”; “will be applied”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; reduced soil erosion; improved watershed protection/water quality; long-term timber supply stability
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a regulatory mandate; classified as a performance-standard because it specifies an outcome condition (extractions ≤ growth) rather than prescribing a specific practice.
